content.text:
## Three-App Supabase Integration Analysis

### 1. Per-App Snapshot

#### **popdam3** — Digital Asset Manager
- **Framework:** React 18 + Vite, served via Coolify/nginx SPA
- **Backend:** Has distributed backend infrastructure:
  - Supabase PostgreSQL (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`) as the primary data store
  - 12 Deno edge functions (`admin-api`, `agent-api`, `helper-api`, `erp-sync`, etc.)
  - Railway cloud worker (Node.js, polling `admin_config.BULK_OPERATIONS` for async jobs: AI tagging, ERP enrichment, style group rebuilds)
  - Bridge agent (Docker on Synology NAS, scans filesystem, generates thumbnails, uploads to DigitalOcean Spaces)
  - Windows render agent (Illustrator batch processing)
- **Supabase access:** Browser directly accesses Supabase via `@supabase/supabase-js` (`externalSupabase` client in `src/lib/external-supabase.ts`). Anon key hardcoded in `src/lib/app-mode.ts` because Lovable overwrites `.env` on every deploy.
- **Data layers:** 
  - Most reads go directly to base tables (`assets`, `style_groups`, `erp_items_current`, etc.) via browser client
  - Writes go through edge functions: `admin-api` (admin operations, scan commands, ERP sync) and `agent-api` (agent heartbeat, ingest, thumbnail upload). Frontend calls edge functions via `supabase.functions.invoke()` with user JWT (see `src/hooks/useAdminApi.ts`)
  - Complex async work (AI tagging, ERP enrichment) runs in Railway worker, not in functions
- **Auth method:** Supabase Auth (Microsoft/Azure primary, Google fallback, email/password legacy, Authentik legacy but hidden). Sessions stored in localStorage as `sb-popdam-auth-token`, auto-refreshed by Supabase client.

#### **popcrm-web** — CRM
- **Framework:** React 19 + Vite, served via Coolify/nginx SPA
- **Backend:** Pure static SPA; all backend is Supabase
- **Supabase access:** Browser accesses Supabase via `@supabase/supabase-js` (`src/lib/supabase.ts`). URL and anon key from env vars `VITE_SUPABASE_URL` and `VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` (shared backend).
- **Data layers:**
  - **Reads:** go through `api.crm_*` views (security_invoker, RLS-enforced). Views are flat denormalized rows; frontend adapters (`src/features/crm/api.ts`) rebuild nested shapes the UI expects. Example: `api.crm_account_list`, `api.crm_contact_segment_list`, `api.crm_contact_segment_counts`.
  - **Writes:** go through guarded RPCs (`api.crm_update_account`, `api.crm_update_contact`) or directly to `crm.*` tables for operational rows (tasks, notes, meetings, etc.). All access via `supabase.schema('api')` or `supabase.schema('crm')` (default schema is `public`).
  - No edge functions, no backend worker, no server-side async jobs
- **Auth method:** Supabase Auth (Microsoft/Azure primary). Sessions auto-refresh via Supabase client. Profile fetched via RPC `api.current_user_profile()`.

#### **poppim-web** — PIM/Project Management
- **Framework:** React 19 + Vite, served via Coolify/nginx SPA
- **Backend:** Pure static SPA; all backend is Supabase
- **Supabase access:** Browser accesses Supabase via `@supabase/supabase-js` (`src/lib/supabase.ts`). URL and anon key from env vars (shared backend).
- **Data layers:**
  - **Reads:** go through Supabase views/tables (`api.pm_product_board`, `pim.*` tables, `core.*` tables for accounts/contacts/users). Reads are denormalized; frontend domain layer (`src/domain/products/supabaseAdapter.ts`) converts Supabase rows into `ProductSummary` view models.
  - **Writes:** direct to `pim.*` tables (workflow records, saved views) and `core.*` tables. Optimistic UI updates reverted on error.
  - No edge functions, no backend worker, no server-side async jobs
- **Auth method:** Supabase Auth (Microsoft/Azure primary). Sessions auto-refresh. Profile fetched via RPC `api.current_user_profile()`.

---

### 2. Direct-Supabase vs. API-Layer

| App | Browser → Supabase | Direct read from base tables? | API/view layer? | Backend-side async? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **popdam3** | Yes, `@supabase/supabase-js` anon key hardcoded | Yes (e.g., `assets`, `style_groups`, `erp_items_current` directly) | Yes: 12 Deno edge functions for async/validated writes; views not primary (mostly base-table reads) | Yes: Railway worker polls `admin_config.BULK_OPERATIONS`, dispatches batch handlers (AI tagging, ERP enrichment, style-group rebuilds). Bridge/Windows agents are separate polling workers. |
| **popcrm-web** | Yes, `@supabase/supabase-js` anon key from env | No; only reads from `api.crm_*` views (security_invoker, RLS-enforced). Never base tables directly. | Yes: `api.` schema views (`crm_account_list`, `crm_contact_segment_list`), guarded RPCs (`crm_update_account`, `crm_update_contact`). Frontend applies pagination and UI adapters. | No backend worker. Writes either go through RPCs or direct to `crm.*` operational tables (baseline RLS allows sales/licensing/admin). |
| **poppim-web** | Yes, `@supabase/supabase-js` anon key from env | Yes (denormalized rows from `api.pm_product_board`, `pim.*`, `core.*`). | Minimal view layer; reads are mostly from denormalized product/workflow tables. Frontend domain adapters hydrate rollups. | No backend worker. All writes direct to Supabase tables or RPCs. |

**Key difference:** PopDAM3 has substantial backend-side async work (Railway worker, Bridge agent, Windows agent) running outside the browser. CRM and PIM are **pure client-side SPA** apps with no backend jobs — all work happens in the browser or in edge function request/response cycles.

---

### 3. Auth Model

All three apps use **Supabase Auth** with **Microsoft/Azure as the primary provider**:

- **popdam3:** Microsoft/Azure primary (no manual invitation required post-2026-06-08), Google + email/password fallback (require invitation), Authentik legacy (hidden). Hardcoded anon key in `src/lib/app-mode.ts`.
- **popcrm-web:** Microsoft/Azure primary (Supabase's built-in Azure provider). Env vars for URL/anon key. RPC `api.current_user_profile()` fetches app profile after Supabase session is valid.
- **poppim-web:** Microsoft/Azure primary. Env vars for URL/anon key. RPC `api.current_user_profile()` fetches app profile.

**Role/claims model:**
- **popdam3:** JWT contains `app_metadata` (provider info); RLS/access checks via `user_roles` table (admin role gates `admin-api`). Agent-api uses separate `x-agent-key` header auth (hashed key compared against `agent_registrations.agent_key_hash`).
- **popcrm-web:** JWT from Supabase Auth; RLS enforced via `app.has_app_access('crm')` policy. Profile row in `app.profile` holds `roles` array.
- **poppim-web:** JWT from Supabase Auth; RLS enforced via `app.has_app_access('pim')` policy. Profile row in `app.profile` holds `roles` array.

**No traditional "API token" layer:** All apps use browser-held Supabase JWT directly. PopDAM3 adds edge functions as a secondary layer to enforce admin-only operations (scanning, ERP sync, config writes), but the browser still holds the user JWT.

---

### 4. RLS Reliance

All three apps depend heavily on **Row-Level Security (RLS)** in the database, since the browser holds the anon/user key:

- **popdam3:** RLS on base tables (`assets`, `style_groups`, etc.) gates visibility by user role. `admin-api` edge function also checks JWT + admin role before executing scans/ERP operations. Agent-api is protected by separate key auth (not RLS).
- **popcrm-web:** RLS on `api.crm_*` views (marked `security_invoker`) enforces app-access checks and data filtering. No view returns secrets (email bodies, transcripts) to the browser. Writes through RPCs or directly to `crm.*` tables with baseline `crm_write` RLS.
- **poppim-web:** RLS on `api.pm_product_board` and related views enforces app-access checks. All reads flow through views; the browser never hits base tables directly.

**Key tension:** In popdam3, the browser can read base tables like `assets` directly, which means RLS must be carefully tuned to prevent leaks. The edge functions add a second validation layer to mitigate, but the core security model is still "browser holds JWT + RLS protects data." CRM and PIM have stricter boundaries (views-only for reads, RPCs for sensitive writes).

---

### 5. Shared-DB Coordination

All three apps reference the **canonical `u2giants/shared-db`** repository as the single source of truth for Supabase schema, migrations, RLS, views, and RPCs:

**References found:**

1. **popdam3:**
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene" (lines 79–117) — mandatory startup/shutdown checklist
   - PopDAM-owned migrations under `supabase/migrations/` follow app-specific discipline
   - Cross-app/shared-schema migrations belong in canonical `/worksp/shared-db`
   - Treats `shared-db/` inside repo as read-only mirror
   - Apps commit to `main`; shared-db uses branch + PR workflow

2. **popcrm-web:**
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene" (lines 67–102) — identical checklist
   - `docs/architecture.md` mentions "the shared Supabase project (`https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co`)" and "shared Supabase/Postgres backend"
   - Data reads go through `api.crm_*` views and `crm_update_*` RPCs (defined in shared-db)
   - Contact field writes reference migration `20260623024500_crm_update_contact_clear_relationship_fields.sql` in shared-db

3. **poppim-web:**
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene" (lines 49–84) — checklist with explicit canonical `/worksp/shared-db` reference
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Documentation map" entry for "Touch the shared Supabase database" points to **`shared-db/AGENTS.md`** (required reading)
   - `docs/architecture.md` states: "Backend schema, roles, RLS, realtime, and migrations are in `u2giants/shared-db`; the local `shared-db/` folder is a read-only synced mirror. Read `shared-db/AGENTS.md` before any database change."

**Migration discipline:**
- Each app repo has a mirrored `shared-db/` folder (auto-synced on pushes to canonical `main`)
- **The four anti-collision rules** (from shared-db AGENTS.md):
  1. One schema change at a time (no parallel changes across apps)
  2. Test on preview branch first (`xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu`), then promote to production (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`)
  3. Additive by default; only rename/drop with explicit approval
  4. New timestamped migration files only; never edit applied migrations

---

### 6. Implications for PLM

**Current state:** PLM (per global instructions) has:
- Heavy Node.js backend (not lightweight edge functions)
- Azure JWT auth (not Supabase Auth)
- Unclear data-access pattern (needs investigation)

**Tensions exposed by analyzing these three apps:**

1. **Browser-direct vs. backend-mediated:**
   - CRM and PIM follow browser-direct pattern: Supabase JWT in browser, RLS + views enforce security.
   - PopDAM3 is mixed: browser-direct for reads, but uses edge functions for sensitive writes (scans, ERP enrichment).
   - **PLM question:** Can PLM use browser-direct Supabase access (matching CRM/PIM), or does the Azure/heavy-backend design require server-mediated access?

2. **Auth provider mismatch:**
   - All three apps use Supabase Auth (Microsoft/Azure as primary provider).
   - PLM uses Azure JWT directly (not Supabase Auth). This means:
     - PLM cannot rely on Supabase Auth refresh/session management.
     - PLM backend must validate Azure JWT and translate it to Supabase session (extra layer).
     - Cross-app user identity must align (same Azure oid/user_id used in shared-db `app.profile.id`).

3. **RLS vs. server-side validation:**
   - CRM/PIM rely entirely on RLS + views (browser-safe, no business logic in views).
   - PopDAM3 adds edge functions as a secondary guard (admin-api checks user role before running scans).
   - **PLM question:** If PLM keeps server-side backend, it can skip RLS on sensitive tables and validate all writes server-side. But this breaks the shared-app pattern — some tables (e.g., accounts, contacts) are used by CRM, which *expects* RLS to work.

4. **Async work pattern:**
   - PopDAM3: Railway worker polls `admin_config` table, dispatches batch jobs server-side.
   - CRM/PIM: All writes are synchronous (direct to Supabase table/RPC).
   - **PLM question:** Does PLM need async background jobs (like PopDAM3), or can it keep all writes synchronous?

5. **API boundary:**
   - PopDAM3: Browser → edge function → Supabase (two-layer, but both are Supabase-hosted).
   - CRM/PIM: Browser → Supabase directly (single layer, RLS enforces security).
   - PLM (current): Browser → PLM backend → Supabase (two-layer, but backend is custom Node.js, not Supabase-hosted).
   - **Tension:** If PLM keeps custom backend, it must coordinate with the shared-db anti-collision rules. Any schema change to shared tables affects PLM, CRM, PIM in parallel.

---

### Summary: Two Viable Paths for PLM

**Path A: Browser-direct (match CRM/PIM):**
- Migrate PLM to Supabase Auth (using Azure as provider, same as other apps).
- Remove or minimize custom Node backend; keep only what cannot run in the browser.
- Reads go through RLS-protected views (`api.plm_*`).
- Writes go through RPCs or direct to `plm.*` tables.
- Join the shared-app coordination model (shared-db branches + PR, four anti-collision rules).
- **Pro:** Simpler, fewer moving parts, aligns with two existing apps.
- **Con:** Requires rewriting PLM auth/backend, high risk if PLM has custom logic that doesn't map to Supabase RLS.

**Path B: Backend-mediated (like PopDAM3's approach):**
- Keep PLM backend (Azure JWT → custom business logic).
- Backend does NOT call Supabase directly; instead:
  - Browser calls PLM backend with Azure JWT.
  - Backend authenticates JWT, then calls Supabase with a **service-role key** (not browser key) OR translates JWT to Supabase session.
  - Backend enforces all business rules, then writes to Supabase.
  - RLS is minimal/bypassed on PLM-owned tables (since all writes come from trusted backend).
- **Pro:** PLM keeps full control of business logic and auth flow. Less risk if current backend has custom patterns.
- **Con:** Breaks the "shared RLS model" — CRM/PIM expect RLS to protect shared tables, but PLM backend writes with elevated privileges. Higher operational complexity.

**Recommendation:** If PLM can be refactored to use Supabase Auth and browser-side RLS (Path A), it will be simpler and safer in the long term. PopDAM3's mixed approach (browser reads + edge-function writes) is only necessary because DAM has heavy async work (AI tagging, thumbnail generation) and agent communication. CRM and PIM prove that pure browser-direct is viable for most business apps.

---

**Files referenced:**
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/AGENTS.md` (lines 79–117, 486–520)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/docs/architecture.md` (lines 1–70, 206–225)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/docs/AUTHENTICATION.md`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/src/lib/external-supabase.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/src/hooks/useAdminApi.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/AGENTS.md` (lines 1–103)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/docs/architecture.md` (lines 1–85)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/src/lib/supabase.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/src/features/crm/api.ts` (lines 1–80)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/shared-db/AGENTS.md` (cross-app coordination playbook, §1–7)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/AGENTS.md` (lines 1–100)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/docs/architecture.md` (lines 1–52)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/src/lib/supabase.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/src/domain/products/supabaseAdapter.ts` (lines 1–100)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/shared-db/AGENTS.md` (cross-app coordination playbook)

  - Windows render agent (Illustrator batch processing)
- **Supabase access:** Browser directly accesses Supabase via `@supabase/supabase-js` (`externalSupabase` client in `src/lib/external-supabase.ts`). Anon key hardcoded in `src/lib/app-mode.ts` because Lovable overwrites `.env` on every deploy.

  - Most reads go directly to base tables (`assets`, `style_groups`, `erp_items_current`, etc.) via browser client
  - Writes go through edge functions: `admin-api` (admin operations, scan commands, ERP sync) and `agent-api` (agent heartbeat, ingest, thumbnail upload). Frontend calls edge functions via `supabase.functions.invoke()` with user JWT (see `src/hooks/useAdminApi.ts`)

- **Data layers:**
  - **Reads:** go through `api.crm_*` views (security_invoker, RLS-enforced). Views are flat denormalized rows; frontend adapters (`src/features/crm/api.ts`) rebuild nested shapes the UI expects. Example: `api.crm_account_list`, `api.crm_contact_segment_list`, `api.crm_contact_segment_counts`.
  - **Writes:** go through guarded RPCs (`api.crm_update_account`, `api.crm_update_contact`) or directly to `crm.*` tables for operational rows (tasks, notes, meetings, etc.). All access via `supabase.schema('api')` or `supabase.schema('crm')` (default schema is `public`).
  - No edge functions, no backend worker, no server-side async jobs
- **Auth method:** Supabase Auth (Microsoft/Azure primary). Sessions auto-refresh via Supabase client. Profile fetched via RPC `api.current_user_profile()`.

#### **poppim-web** — PIM/Project Management
- **Framework:** React 19 + Vite, served via Coolify/nginx SPA
- **Backend:** Pure static SPA; all backend is Supabase
- **Supabase access:** Browser accesses Supabase via `@supabase/supabase-js` (`src/lib/supabase.ts`). URL and anon key from env vars (shared backend).
- **Data layers:**
  - **Reads:** go through Supabase views/tables (`api.pm_product_board`, `pim.*` tables, `core.*` tables for accounts/contacts/users). Reads are denormalized; frontend domain layer (`src/domain/products/supabaseAdapter.ts`) converts Supabase rows into `ProductSummary` view models.
  - **Writes:** direct to `pim.*` tables (workflow records, saved views) and `core.*` tables. Optimistic UI updates reverted on error.
  - No edge functions, no backend worker, no server-side async jobs
- **Auth method:** Supabase Auth (Microsoft/Azure primary). Sessions auto-refresh. Profile fetched via RPC `api.current_user_profile()`.

---

### 2. Direct-Supabase vs. API-Layer

| App | Browser → Supabase | Direct read from base tables? | API/view layer? | Backend-side async? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **popdam3** | Yes, `@supabase/supabase-js` anon key hardcoded | Yes (e.g., `assets`, `style_groups`, `erp_items_current` directly) | Yes: 12 Deno edge functions for async/validated writes; views not primary (mostly base-table reads) | Yes: Railway worker polls `admin_config.BULK_OPERATIONS`, dispatches batch handlers (AI tagging, ERP enrichment, style-group rebuilds). Bridge/Windows agents are separate polling workers. |
| **popcrm-web** | Yes, `@supabase/supabase-js` anon key from env | No; only reads from `api.crm_*` views (security_invoker, RLS-enforced). Never base tables directly. | Yes: `api.` schema views (`crm_account_list`, `crm_contact_segment_list`), guarded RPCs (`crm_update_account`, `crm_update_contact`). Frontend applies pagination and UI adapters. | No backend worker. Writes either go through RPCs or direct to `crm.*` operational tables (baseline RLS allows sales/licensing/admin). |
| **poppim-web** | Yes, `@supabase/supabase-js` anon key from env | Yes (denormalized rows from `api.pm_product_board`, `pim.*`, `core.*`). | Minimal view layer; reads are mostly from denormalized product/workflow tables. Frontend domain adapters hydrate rollups. | No backend worker. All writes direct to Supabase tables or RPCs. |

**Key difference:** PopDAM3 has substantial backend-side async work (Railway worker, Bridge agent, Windows agent) running outside the browser. CRM and PIM are **pure client-side SPA** apps with no backend jobs — all work happens in the browser or in edge function request/response cycles.

---

### 3. Auth Model

All three apps use **Supabase Auth** with **Microsoft/Azure as the primary provider**:

- **popdam3:** Microsoft/Azure primary (no manual invitation required post-2026-06-08), Google + email/password fallback (require invitation), Authentik legacy (hidden). Hardcoded anon key in `src/lib/app-mode.ts`.
- **popcrm-web:** Microsoft/Azure primary (Supabase's built-in Azure provider). Env vars for URL/anon key. RPC `api.current_user_profile()` fetches app profile after Supabase session is valid.
- **poppim-web:** Microsoft/Azure primary. Env vars for URL/anon key. RPC `api.current_user_profile()` fetches app profile.

**Role/claims model:**
- **popdam3:** JWT contains `app_metadata` (provider info); RLS/access checks via `user_roles` table (admin role gates `admin-api`). Agent-api uses separate `x-agent-key` header auth (hashed key compared against `agent_registrations.agent_key_hash`).
- **popcrm-web:** JWT from Supabase Auth; RLS enforced via `app.has_app_access('crm')` policy. Profile row in `app.profile` holds `roles` array.
- **poppim-web:** JWT from Supabase Auth; RLS enforced via `app.has_app_access('pim')` policy. Profile row in `app.profile` holds `roles` array.

**No traditional "API token" layer:** All apps use browser-held Supabase JWT directly. PopDAM3 adds edge functions as a secondary layer to enforce admin-only operations (scanning, ERP sync, config writes), but the browser still holds the user JWT.

---

### 4. RLS Reliance

All three apps depend heavily on **Row-Level Security (RLS)** in the database, since the browser holds the anon/user key:

- **popdam3:** RLS on base tables (`assets`, `style_groups`, etc.) gates visibility by user role. `admin-api` edge function also checks JWT + admin role before executing scans/ERP operations. Agent-api is protected by separate key auth (not RLS).
- **popcrm-web:** RLS on `api.crm_*` views (marked `security_invoker`) enforces app-access checks and data filtering. No view returns secrets (email bodies, transcripts) to the browser. Writes through RPCs or directly to `crm.*` tables with baseline `crm_write` RLS.
- **poppim-web:** RLS on `api.pm_product_board` and related views enforces app-access checks. All reads flow through views; the browser never hits base tables directly.

**Key tension:** In popdam3, the browser can read base tables like `assets` directly, which means RLS must be carefully tuned to prevent leaks. The edge functions add a second validation layer to mitigate, but the core security model is still "browser holds JWT + RLS protects data." CRM and PIM have stricter boundaries (views-only for reads, RPCs for sensitive writes).

---

### 5. Shared-DB Coordination

All three apps reference the **canonical `u2giants/shared-db`** repository as the single source of truth for Supabase schema, migrations, RLS, views, and RPCs:

**References found:**

1. **popdam3:**
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene" (lines 79–117) — mandatory startup/shutdown checklist
   - PopDAM-owned migrations under `supabase/migrations/` follow app-specific discipline
   - Cross-app/shared-schema migrations belong in canonical `/worksp/shared-db`
   - Treats `shared-db/` inside repo as read-only mirror
   - Apps commit to `main`; shared-db uses branch + PR workflow

2. **popcrm-web:**
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene" (lines 67–102) — identical checklist
   - `docs/architecture.md` mentions "the shared Supabase project (`https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co`)" and "shared Supabase/Postgres backend"
   - Data reads go through `api.crm_*` views and `crm_update_*` RPCs (defined in shared-db)
   - Contact field writes reference migration `20260623024500_crm_update_contact_clear_relationship_fields.sql` in shared-db

3. **poppim-web:**
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene" (lines 49–84) — checklist with explicit canonical `/worksp/shared-db` reference
   - `AGENTS.md` §"Documentation map" entry for "Touch the shared Supabase database" points to **`shared-db/AGENTS.md`** (required reading)
   - `docs/architecture.md` states: "Backend schema, roles, RLS, realtime, and migrations are in `u2giants/shared-db`; the local `shared-db/` folder is a read-only synced mirror. Read `shared-db/AGENTS.md` before any database change."

**Migration discipline:**
- Each app repo has a mirrored `shared-db/` folder (auto-synced on pushes to canonical `main`)
- **The four anti-collision rules** (from shared-db AGENTS.md):
  1. One schema change at a time (no parallel changes across apps)
  2. Test on preview branch first (`xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu`), then promote to production (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`)
  3. Additive by default; only rename/drop with explicit approval
  4. New timestamped migration files only; never edit applied migrations

---

### 6. Implications for PLM

**Current state:** PLM (per global instructions) has:
- Heavy Node.js backend (not lightweight edge functions)
- Azure JWT auth (not Supabase Auth)
- Unclear data-access pattern (needs investigation)

**Tensions exposed by analyzing these three apps:**

1. **Browser-direct vs. backend-mediated:**
   - CRM and PIM follow browser-direct pattern: Supabase JWT in browser, RLS + views enforce security.
   - PopDAM3 is mixed: browser-direct for reads, but uses edge functions for sensitive writes (scans, ERP enrichment).
   - **PLM question:** Can PLM use browser-direct Supabase access (matching CRM/PIM), or does the Azure/heavy-backend design require server-mediated access?

2. **Auth provider mismatch:**
   - All three apps use Supabase Auth (Microsoft/Azure as primary provider).
   - PLM uses Azure JWT directly (not Supabase Auth). This means:
     - PLM cannot rely on Supabase Auth refresh/session management.
     - PLM backend must validate Azure JWT and translate it to Supabase session (extra layer).
     - Cross-app user identity must align (same Azure oid/user_id used in shared-db `app.profile.id`).

3. **RLS vs. server-side validation:**
   - CRM/PIM rely entirely on RLS + views (browser-safe, no business logic in views).
   - PopDAM3 adds edge functions as a secondary guard (admin-api checks user role before running scans).
   - **PLM question:** If PLM keeps server-side backend, it can skip RLS on sensitive tables and validate all writes server-side. But this breaks the shared-app pattern — some tables (e.g., accounts, contacts) are used by CRM, which *expects* RLS to work.

4. **Async work pattern:**
   - PopDAM3: Railway worker polls `admin_config` table, dispatches batch jobs server-side.
   - CRM/PIM: All writes are synchronous (direct to Supabase table/RPC).
   - **PLM question:** Does PLM need async background jobs (like PopDAM3), or can it keep all writes synchronous?

5. **API boundary:**
   - PopDAM3: Browser → edge function → Supabase (two-layer, but both are Supabase-hosted).
   - CRM/PIM: Browser → Supabase directly (single layer, RLS enforces security).
   - PLM (current): Browser → PLM backend → Supabase (two-layer, but backend is custom Node.js, not Supabase-hosted).
   - **Tension:** If PLM keeps custom backend, it must coordinate with the shared-db anti-collision rules. Any schema change to shared tables affects PLM, CRM, PIM in parallel.

---

### Summary: Two Viable Paths for PLM

**Path A: Browser-direct (match CRM/PIM):**
- Migrate PLM to Supabase Auth (using Azure as provider, same as other apps).
- Remove or minimize custom Node backend; keep only what cannot run in the browser.
- Reads go through RLS-protected views (`api.plm_*`).
- Writes go through RPCs or direct to `plm.*` tables.
- Join the shared-app coordination model (shared-db branches + PR, four anti-collision rules).
- **Pro:** Simpler, fewer moving parts, aligns with two existing apps.
- **Con:** Requires rewriting PLM auth/backend, high risk if PLM has custom logic that doesn't map to Supabase RLS.

**Path B: Backend-mediated (like PopDAM3's approach):**
- Keep PLM backend (Azure JWT → custom business logic).
- Backend does NOT call Supabase directly; instead:
  - Browser calls PLM backend with Azure JWT.
  - Backend authenticates JWT, then calls Supabase with a **service-role key** (not browser key) OR translates JWT to Supabase session.
  - Backend enforces all business rules, then writes to Supabase.
  - RLS is minimal/bypassed on PLM-owned tables (since all writes come from trusted backend).
- **Pro:** PLM keeps full control of business logic and auth flow. Less risk if current backend has custom patterns.
- **Con:** Breaks the "shared RLS model" — CRM/PIM expect RLS to protect shared tables, but PLM backend writes with elevated privileges. Higher operational complexity.

**Recommendation:** If PLM can be refactored to use Supabase Auth and browser-side RLS (Path A), it will be simpler and safer in the long term. PopDAM3's mixed approach (browser reads + edge-function writes) is only necessary because DAM has heavy async work (AI tagging, thumbnail generation) and agent communication. CRM and PIM prove that pure browser-direct is viable for most business apps.

---

**Files referenced:**
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/AGENTS.md` (lines 79–117, 486–520)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/docs/architecture.md` (lines 1–70, 206–225)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/docs/AUTHENTICATION.md`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/src/lib/external-supabase.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3/src/hooks/useAdminApi.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/AGENTS.md` (lines 1–103)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/docs/architecture.md` (lines 1–85)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/src/lib/supabase.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/src/features/crm/api.ts` (lines 1–80)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web/shared-db/AGENTS.md` (cross-app coordination playbook, §1–7)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/AGENTS.md` (lines 1–100)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/docs/architecture.md` (lines 1–52)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/src/lib/supabase.ts`
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/src/domain/products/supabaseAdapter.ts` (lines 1–100)
- `C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web/shared-db/AGENTS.md` (cross-app coordination playbook)

input.content:
# Designflow PLM → Shared Supabase Migration Plan

> Status: **DRAFT — discovery complete, awaiting key decisions (Part 4).**
> Date started: 2026-06-23
> Target Supabase project: `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` (production) / preview branch `xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu`
> Owner contract: this plan obeys [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) — the four anti-collision rules for the shared database.

---

## Part 1 — The mandate (background, as given)

Migrate the very large **Designflow PLM backend** from **GCP Cloud SQL** to **hosted supabase.com**, into the **existing shared project** `https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co`. This Supabase project is a **shared database for four applications** that share a lot of data, so one database makes sense:

- **PLM** — Designflow product lifecycle management (the system being migrated here).
- **DAM** — digital asset management (`popdam3`).
- **CRM** — companies, contacts, opportunities, communications (`popcrm-web`).
- **PM/PIM** — projects, products, designs, workflow (`poppim-web`).

This is **not** a migration of *all* of PLM's backend, and it is **not** *only* the database (that would defeat the purpose of Supabase). We must find the **perfect balance in the middle**. Some of the backend with heavy logic (and there is a lot of it) should **stay in code** and not move to Supabase.

Everything below must be planned for:

- **Migration changes how services access data, auth, APIs, and possibly storage.**
- **RLS design becomes critical.** Multiple services and clients hit Supabase APIs; bad RLS policies cause security bugs or broken access.
- **Service boundaries can blur.** If every service reads/writes shared tables through Supabase APIs, business logic scatters.
- **Governance is required.** Clear rules for table ownership, schema changes, migrations, access policies, service roles, and audit logging.
- **The custom backend still matters.** Designflow services hold business logic, integrations, Azure JWT auth, emails, AI proxying, file workflows, and domain rules. Supabase will not replace all of that immediately.
- **Performance must be tested.** Direct Supabase APIs are convenient but not always better than optimized backend endpoints for complex queries, grids, reporting, or bulk workflows.
- **Connection and rate limits matter.** Multiple services using Supabase APIs and DB connections need proper pooling, service keys, quotas, and monitoring.

We must build **guardrails that prevent coding on one program from breaking another program**, study the Designflow application to decide **what to migrate and in what stages**, and execute in a **controlled, thought-out way with extensive testing at each stage**.

---

## Part 2 — Current-state architecture (discovery findings)

Discovery was performed on **2026-06-23** by reading all `.md` files and the codebases of the nine repos on their stated branches (PLM repos on `sandbox-albert`; the three web apps on `main`).

### 2.1 There are two databases today

| Database | Engine | Schema | Who uses it | How |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLM Cloud SQL** | GCP Cloud SQL Postgres | `designflow` | The 6 `popcre/designflow-*` services | Sequelize ORM over TCP (VPC connector) |
| **Shared Supabase** | Supabase Postgres `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` | `app`, `core`, `dam`, `crm`, `pim`, `plm`, `ingest`, `api`, `public` | DAM, CRM, PM/PIM browsers | `@supabase/supabase-js` direct, RLS + `api.*` views/RPCs |

The shared-db planning docs **already reserve a `plm` schema** and map PLM tables into it (see [`docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md`](docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md)). This migration is the act of **filling that `plm` schema with real PLM data and repointing the PLM services at Supabase**.

### 2.2 The six PLM services (all on GCP Cloud Run)

| Service | Role | DB? | Auth | Heavy logic / integrations | Migration weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **designflow-bff** | Pure API gateway / proxy. Routes `/api/{core,item_master,tracking,data_sync}/*` to the four backends. Mints Cloud Run OIDC tokens; forwards user JWT as `X-User-Authorization`. | **None** | Validates nothing itself; forwards JWT. Optional `X-API-Key`→JWT. | Cloud Run OIDC, CORS, API-key auth. | **Zero DB change.** Role unchanged. |
| **designflow-backend** (core) | RFQ/quoting, products, customers, vendors, tariffs, admin, notifications, comments, **auth (Azure SSO → mints Designflow RS256 JWT)**. ~67 Sequelize models, ~150 endpoints. | Yes | **Azure AD/Entra** token verified via JWKS → mints own **RS256 JWT (12h)**; `authRole()` middleware. | RFQ pricing/quote state machine, re-quote workflow, stale-quote auto-archive, Excel bulk import, tariff cascades (Section 301 / HTS / Trump 2025), **AI proxy (OpenRouter/DeepSeek/Qwen)**, **email (Brevo)**, **MS Graph photos**, **DigitalOcean Spaces** files, **CBP CROSS** proxy. | **Heavy. Stays in code.** Repoint DB only. |
| **designflow-item-master** | Item library/detail, art pieces, licensing status/feedback/milestones, product-user assignments. ~34 models. | Yes | Designflow RS256 JWT, `authRole()`. | Item-number generation (bounded retry), licensing lifecycle, **Teams notifications (synchronous)**. | Medium. Repoint DB. |
| **designflow-tracking** | Production tracking, licensing tracking, **lead-time calc**, **sample tracking** (new on `sandbox-albert`: `sample`, `sample_event`, `sample_comments`, `sample_attachment`, `sample_factory_group`, `sample_box`, `sample_shipment_item`). ~35 models. | Yes | Designflow RS256 JWT, `authRole()`. | Sample **state machine** (`helpers/sampleStatus.js`), lead-time startup caches, AG-Grid server-side pagination, DigitalOcean Spaces photos. **Already tuned for the Supabase pooler** (`pool.min:0, idle:10s, evict:5s, keepAlive:true`) — see [`docs/verification/supabase-pooler-idle-connection-drop-20260623.md`](docs/verification/supabase-pooler-idle-connection-drop-20260623.md). | Medium. **Best-prepared service.** Repoint DB. |
| **designflow-data-syncing** | **ETL: pulls Coldlion ERP → PLM Postgres** (item headers/details, merch groups, production orders, external vendors/customers, prepack associations). `modTime`-guarded upserts. | Yes (write) | Inbound `X-API-Key`; outbound `COLDLION_API_KEY`. Invoked by host systemd `plm-sync.timer` (Ansible-managed). | Field remapping, dedup/find-or-create by composite keys, **image upload to GCS** (`storage.googleapis.com/designflow-default-bucket`). | Medium. Repoint DB; storage decision needed. |
| **designflow-frontend** | Angular 21 SPA. AG Grid Enterprise. **MSAL Azure SSO** popup → exchanges Azure token at core for Designflow JWT (stored in `localStorage`). Calls everything through the BFF. **No `@supabase/supabase-js` today.** | n/a | Azure SSO + Designflow JWT in `Authorization`. | Client-side RFQ margin/landed-cost math (`rfq.component.ts` ~4500 lines, `equation.ts`), reference-data cache, presigned-URL uploads. | Consumer only. Changes only if we expose Supabase to it. |

**Key facts that shape the plan:**

- **All PLM DB access is standard Postgres + Sequelize.** There is **no Cloud SQL IAM/proxy/socket coupling** — repointing to Supabase is a connection-string + pool-config change, not a code rewrite. The only true GCP couplings are: Cloud Build/Cloud Run deploy, Secret Manager, VPC connector, and **data-syncing's GCS image uploads**.
- **Model duplication across repos is a real hazard.** `itemHeader`, `itemDetail`, `ProdOrderHeader/Detail`, `Factory`, `vendor`, `users`, `groups`, `merchGroup`, grid tables, etc. are each **defined separately in multiple services' Sequelize models**. Columns can drift between copies. Supabase DDL must be the **union of all columns** across every repo's copy of a model, or an ORM in one service will reference a column another service didn't create. (Already flagged in [`docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md`](docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md) line 84–86.)
- **PLM auth is a different identity system.** PLM mints its **own RS256 JWT** with `{user_id, email, role}` where role ∈ {admin, sales, designer, production, sourcing_manager, vendor}. The other three apps use **Supabase Auth** (Azure provider) and RLS keyed on `app.profile`/`app.has_app_access(...)`. These identities must be reconciled before RLS can protect any table PLM writes and another app reads.
- **Connection-limit math is a risk.** Six services, each `pool.max ≈ 22`, each running multiple Cloud Run instances, all against one Supabase project, plus three browser apps. This will exceed direct-connection limits; the **Supabase transaction pooler (pgBouncer)** and disciplined pool sizing are mandatory. The idle-connection-drop issue is already documented and fixed in `tracking`; the same settings must be applied to every service.
- **A known auth bug exists** in `designflow-backend/helpers/auth.js` (`algorithm` vs `algorithms` — the RS256 allow-list is not enforced, enabling forged-token bypass). This must be fixed **before** PLM identity is trusted by shared RLS. There is also a leaked Brevo key in git history to rotate.

### 2.3 How the other three apps use the shared DB (the pattern PLM is joining)

- **CRM (`popcrm-web`)** and **PM (`poppim-web`)** are **pure browser→Supabase SPAs**: reads through `api.*` views (`security_invoker`, RLS-enforced), writes through guarded RPCs or directly to their schema's tables. No server backend. Supabase Auth (Azure). They depend **entirely on RLS** for security.
- **DAM (`popdam3`)** is **mixed**: browser reads base tables directly, but sensitive writes/async go through **Deno edge functions** + a **Railway worker** + filesystem agents. Supabase Auth.
- All three treat `u2giants/shared-db` as the **single source of truth**, carry a read-only synced `shared-db/` mirror, and follow the **four anti-collision rules** in `AGENTS.md`.

**Implication for PLM:** PLM is the only one of the four with a heavy custom Node backend and its own JWT. It will not become a pure browser-direct app soon. The realistic near-term shape is **backend-mediated** (PLM services keep the business logic and connect to Supabase), while **shared identity tables** (companies/contacts/factories/licensors) must still be RLS-safe because CRM/PM read them browser-direct.

---

## Part 3 — Guiding principles (the "perfect balance")

### 3.1 What MOVES to Supabase
- **The data itself** — PLM tables land in the `plm` schema (and selected master data is reconciled into `core`).
- **Cross-app read contracts** — stable `api.plm_*` views/RPCs so CRM/PM/DAM can read canonical PLM item/order/licensing status without touching PLM's Node services or raw tables.
- **Row-level security** for any table exposed to a browser (directly or via `api` views).
- **Realtime** — only for user-facing status tables, and only after RLS is proven.
- **Light, set-based integrity** that is genuinely data-shaped (FKs, simple triggers for audit/`updated_at`, generated columns) — where it does not duplicate app logic.

### 3.2 What STAYS in the Node backend (do not move into Supabase RLS/Edge Functions)
- **RFQ pricing & quote state machine**, re-quote workflow, stale-quote auto-archive (`designflow-backend`).
- **Tariff cascades** (Section 301 / HTS / Trump 2025) and **AI proxying** (OpenRouter/DeepSeek/Qwen, prompt-cache routing).
- **Email (Brevo)**, **Teams/Graph notifications**, **CBP CROSS** proxy.
- **Excel bulk import**, image processing, presigned-URL file workflows.
- **Coldlion ETL** transform/dedup/reconciliation (`designflow-data-syncing`).
- **Sample-tracking state machine** and **lead-time calculation**.
- **Azure SSO exchange** and any privileged orchestration.

> Rule of thumb: **data and access-control go to Supabase; procedural/integration/business logic stays in code.** If logic needs to call an external API, send email, or run a multi-step workflow, it stays in Node.

### 3.3 Cross-app guardrails (so PLM work can't break CRM/PM/DAM, and vice-versa)
1. **Schema ownership is explicit.** PLM owns `plm.*`. Shared identity lives in `core.*` and is changed only by additive, reviewed migrations. No app writes another app's schema except through an agreed contract.
2. **All schema changes follow `AGENTS.md`**: one change in flight at a time; preview branch first; additive-by-default (expand→contract); new timestamped migration files only; `shared-db` branch+PR+merge protocol.
3. **PLM services connect with a scoped role**, not a god key. A dedicated Postgres role/`search_path` per service, least-privilege grants, and (where browser-exposed) RLS — never the service-role key in a browser.
4. **Contract tests** guard the `api.plm_*` views/RPCs: a change that breaks the shape a consumer relies on fails CI before merge.
5. **Column-parity check** across the duplicated Sequelize models, run in CI, so one service can't add a column the shared DDL lacks.
6. **Source-reference tables** (`core.*_source_ref`) preserve every original PLM id so links are reversible and dedup is auditable.

---

## Part 4 — Key decisions required before locking the plan

These four decisions change the **shape** of the staged plan. They are posed to the owner now; the rest of the open questions (Part 6) can be resolved as we go.

1. **End state of Cloud SQL** — Does Supabase *fully replace* PLM's Cloud SQL (the six services repoint and Cloud SQL is decommissioned after cutover), or does Cloud SQL stay primary while Supabase holds a *synced shared copy* for cross-app reads?
2. **PLM access pattern & identity** — Keep PLM **backend-mediated** (Node services hold the logic, connect to Supabase via a scoped service role, identity bridged to Supabase Auth) — or invest now in moving PLM toward **browser-direct** like CRM/PM?
3. **Backend hosting** — Keep the six services on **GCP Cloud Run** (repoint DB to Supabase over the public endpoint + pooler) — or move them to **Coolify/Hetzner** alongside the other apps to cut DB latency and unify ops?
4. **Canonical ownership of shared identity** (customers, contacts, factories, licensors, taxonomy) — In stage one, keep these **PLM-local in `plm.*` and link to `core.*` via source-refs** (lower risk, faster) — or **merge into `core.*` immediately** (the grand vision, higher risk)?

---

## Part 5 — Staged migration plan (DRAFT — refined once Part 4 is answered)

Every stage ends with an explicit **test gate**; no stage promotes to production until its gate passes on the preview branch and the owner confirms behavior. Stages are serialized per the anti-collision rules.

### Stage 0 — Foundations & safety (no data movement)
- Fix the **JWT algorithm bug** in `designflow-backend/helpers/auth.js`; rotate the leaked **Brevo** key.
- Produce a **column-level inventory** of every Sequelize model across all six repos; diff duplicated models; define the **union DDL** per shared table.
- Take a fresh **Cloud SQL dump** (custom-format) and stand up a **disposable rehearsal target** (Supabase preview branch).
- Decide and document the **service Postgres roles**, `search_path`, and pooler strategy (transaction pooler; pool sizes sized to Supabase limits).
- **Gate:** auth bug fixed + tests green; rehearsal restore succeeds; column-parity diff reviewed; role/pooling design approved.

### Stage 1 — Schema landing in `plm` (additive, no cutover)
- Author migrations that create the **`plm.*` tables** as the union of all PLM models, on the preview branch, via the `shared-db` repo (branch + PR + merge protocol).
- Create **`core.*_source_ref`** tables to preserve PLM ids.
- Do **not** repoint any service yet. PLM still runs on Cloud SQL.
- **Gate:** `supabase db push --dry-run` clean; schema applied to preview; `pg_restore`-loaded PLM data validates (row counts, FKs, encoding) against the preview `plm` schema.

### Stage 2 — Repoint the lowest-risk service (pilot)
- Pilot with **`designflow-tracking`** (already pooler-tuned; `sample` tracking is self-contained and not yet in production per the verification note) **or** **`designflow-data-syncing`** (isolated, API-key only) — chosen in refinement.
- Repoint that one service's connection to the Supabase preview project; apply the standard pool settings; run its unit suite + smoke tests under load.
- **Gate:** pilot service fully functional against Supabase preview; pooler behavior (no idle-drop stalls) verified under realistic idle/burst patterns; performance compared to Cloud SQL baseline.

### Stage 3 — Repoint remaining services
- Repoint **item-master**, then **backend (core)**, serializing each with its own gate. The **BFF needs no change**. The **frontend** keeps calling the BFF.
- Apply identical pool settings everywhere; watch the **aggregate connection count** against Supabase limits.
- **Gate:** each service green against preview; end-to-end RFQ / item / licensing / production / sample flows verified through the BFF; connection-count headroom confirmed.

### Stage 4 — Cross-app read contracts + RLS
- Build **`api.plm_*` views/RPCs** for the canonical PLM data CRM/PM/DAM need (item status, production-order status, licensing status), with **RLS** and pricing/vendor-field protection.
- Reconcile shared identity per the Part-4 decision (source-refs first, or merge into `core`).
- Bridge **PLM identity ↔ Supabase Auth** so RLS predicates resolve for the same user across apps.
- **Gate:** RLS verified per role with real test users (incl. vendor row-scoping and pricing-field hiding); CRM/PM read PLM contracts on preview without regressions; no sensitive field leaks.

### Stage 5 — Production cutover
- Final delta sync / write-freeze window; flip each service's secrets from Cloud SQL to the **production** Supabase project; promote schema from preview to production in an approved window.
- Keep Cloud SQL **read-only as a fallback** for an agreed period.
- **Gate:** production smoke tests across all four apps; rollback rehearsed (revert connection secrets); monitoring/alerting on connections, latency, and error rates live.

### Stage 6 — Realtime, optimization & decommission
- Enable **realtime** selectively on user-facing PLM status tables (after RLS).
- Decide per-grid whether to keep optimized backend endpoints or expose direct Supabase reads (performance-tested).
- Storage decision for data-syncing (GCS vs Spaces vs Supabase Storage).
- Decommission Cloud SQL once stable.
- **Gate:** realtime correct under RLS; performance acceptable on the heavy grids; storage migrated or explicitly deferred; Cloud SQL retired.

---

## Part 6 — Open questions (resolve during refinement)

- **Coldlion ETL:** post-migration it pulls into Supabase `plm` directly — confirm. Does any master data it ingests (customers/vendors/merch groups) get reconciled into `core`, or stay `plm` and link via source-ref?
- **Storage:** keep DigitalOcean Spaces (backend/tracking) and GCS (data-syncing) as-is during DB migration (recommended), or unify later? Hardcoded `storage.googleapis.com` URLs in data-syncing need a plan if storage moves.
- **Sample tracking:** it is on `sandbox-albert` but the verification note says the `/sample` backend isn't on `develop`/production yet. Is it in scope for this migration or deferred?
- **Region/latency:** which Supabase region, relative to GCP Cloud Run region (if services stay on Cloud Run)? Cross-region DB round-trips will hurt the heavy grids.
- **Connection budget:** target Supabase plan/tier and its pooler connection ceiling, so we can size `pool.max × instances × services` safely.
- **Write-freeze window:** acceptable maintenance window for final cutover.
- **Audit logging:** keep app-driven Sequelize `AuditLog`, or move to DB triggers? (Recommend keep app-driven initially.)
- **Branch reconciliation:** PLM discovery used `sandbox-albert`; the existing shared-db schema map used `main`. Which PLM branch is the migration's source of truth?
- **Directus overlap:** PM/CRM/DAM data originated from a Directus-owned Postgres; PLM is separate Cloud SQL. Confirm there is no expectation that PLM data flows *through* Directus.

---

## Part 7 — Risks (top of mind)

| Risk | Why it bites | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Model column drift across repos | One service references a column the shared DDL lacks → runtime ORM error | Column-parity CI check; union DDL; Stage 0 inventory |
| Connection-limit exhaustion | 6 services × pool × instances + 3 browser apps on one project | Transaction pooler; disciplined `pool.max`; monitoring; the documented idle-drop settings everywhere |
| RLS gaps on shared tables | CRM/PM read `core`/`api` browser-direct; a bad policy leaks pricing/vendor data | RLS-first, role-tested with real users; pricing behind views; vendor row-scoping before vendor access |
| Auth identity mismatch | PLM JWT ≠ Supabase Auth → RLS can't identify the user | Bridge identity to `app.profile`; fix JWT bug first |
| Breaking another app via shared schema | Four apps, one DB, separate AI sessions | `AGENTS.md` four rules; one change in flight; preview-first; additive-by-default; contract tests |
| Performance regression on heavy grids | Direct Supabase / cross-region latency vs tuned endpoints | Keep backend endpoints for heavy grids; performance-test before exposing direct reads |
| Storage coupling (GCS hardcoded URLs) | data-syncing writes GCS; cutover could orphan images | Keep storage as-is during DB migration; separate storage rehearsal later |

---

*Next: resolve Part 4, then refine Part 5 into concrete migrations, role definitions, and per-stage test checklists.*

toolUseResult.content:
# Designflow PLM → Shared Supabase Migration Plan

> Status: **DRAFT — discovery complete, awaiting key decisions (Part 4).**
> Date started: 2026-06-23
> Target Supabase project: `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` (production) / preview branch `xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu`
> Owner contract: this plan obeys [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) — the four anti-collision rules for the shared database.

---

## Part 1 — The mandate (background, as given)

Migrate the very large **Designflow PLM backend** from **GCP Cloud SQL** to **hosted supabase.com**, into the **existing shared project** `https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co`. This Supabase project is a **shared database for four applications** that share a lot of data, so one database makes sense:

- **PLM** — Designflow product lifecycle management (the system being migrated here).
- **DAM** — digital asset management (`popdam3`).
- **CRM** — companies, contacts, opportunities, communications (`popcrm-web`).
- **PM/PIM** — projects, products, designs, workflow (`poppim-web`).

This is **not** a migration of *all* of PLM's backend, and it is **not** *only* the database (that would defeat the purpose of Supabase). We must find the **perfect balance in the middle**. Some of the backend with heavy logic (and there is a lot of it) should **stay in code** and not move to Supabase.

Everything below must be planned for:

- **Migration changes how services access data, auth, APIs, and possibly storage.**
- **RLS design becomes critical.** Multiple services and clients hit Supabase APIs; bad RLS policies cause security bugs or broken access.
- **Service boundaries can blur.** If every service reads/writes shared tables through Supabase APIs, business logic scatters.
- **Governance is required.** Clear rules for table ownership, schema changes, migrations, access policies, service roles, and audit logging.
- **The custom backend still matters.** Designflow services hold business logic, integrations, Azure JWT auth, emails, AI proxying, file workflows, and domain rules. Supabase will not replace all of that immediately.
- **Performance must be tested.** Direct Supabase APIs are convenient but not always better than optimized backend endpoints for complex queries, grids, reporting, or bulk workflows.
- **Connection and rate limits matter.** Multiple services using Supabase APIs and DB connections need proper pooling, service keys, quotas, and monitoring.

We must build **guardrails that prevent coding on one program from breaking another program**, study the Designflow application to decide **what to migrate and in what stages**, and execute in a **controlled, thought-out way with extensive testing at each stage**.

---

## Part 2 — Current-state architecture (discovery findings)

Discovery was performed on **2026-06-23** by reading all `.md` files and the codebases of the nine repos on their stated branches (PLM repos on `sandbox-albert`; the three web apps on `main`).

### 2.1 There are two databases today

| Database | Engine | Schema | Who uses it | How |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLM Cloud SQL** | GCP Cloud SQL Postgres | `designflow` | The 6 `popcre/designflow-*` services | Sequelize ORM over TCP (VPC connector) |
| **Shared Supabase** | Supabase Postgres `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` | `app`, `core`, `dam`, `crm`, `pim`, `plm`, `ingest`, `api`, `public` | DAM, CRM, PM/PIM browsers | `@supabase/supabase-js` direct, RLS + `api.*` views/RPCs |

The shared-db planning docs **already reserve a `plm` schema** and map PLM tables into it (see [`docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md`](docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md)). This migration is the act of **filling that `plm` schema with real PLM data and repointing the PLM services at Supabase**.

### 2.2 The six PLM services (all on GCP Cloud Run)

| Service | Role | DB? | Auth | Heavy logic / integrations | Migration weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **designflow-bff** | Pure API gateway / proxy. Routes `/api/{core,item_master,tracking,data_sync}/*` to the four backends. Mints Cloud Run OIDC tokens; forwards user JWT as `X-User-Authorization`. | **None** | Validates nothing itself; forwards JWT. Optional `X-API-Key`→JWT. | Cloud Run OIDC, CORS, API-key auth. | **Zero DB change.** Role unchanged. |
| **designflow-backend** (core) | RFQ/quoting, products, customers, vendors, tariffs, admin, notifications, comments, **auth (Azure SSO → mints Designflow RS256 JWT)**. ~67 Sequelize models, ~150 endpoints. | Yes | **Azure AD/Entra** token verified via JWKS → mints own **RS256 JWT (12h)**; `authRole()` middleware. | RFQ pricing/quote state machine, re-quote workflow, stale-quote auto-archive, Excel bulk import, tariff cascades (Section 301 / HTS / Trump 2025), **AI proxy (OpenRouter/DeepSeek/Qwen)**, **email (Brevo)**, **MS Graph photos**, **DigitalOcean Spaces** files, **CBP CROSS** proxy. | **Heavy. Stays in code.** Repoint DB only. |
| **designflow-item-master** | Item library/detail, art pieces, licensing status/feedback/milestones, product-user assignments. ~34 models. | Yes | Designflow RS256 JWT, `authRole()`. | Item-number generation (bounded retry), licensing lifecycle, **Teams notifications (synchronous)**. | Medium. Repoint DB. |
| **designflow-tracking** | Production tracking, licensing tracking, **lead-time calc**, **sample tracking** (new on `sandbox-albert`: `sample`, `sample_event`, `sample_comments`, `sample_attachment`, `sample_factory_group`, `sample_box`, `sample_shipment_item`). ~35 models. | Yes | Designflow RS256 JWT, `authRole()`. | Sample **state machine** (`helpers/sampleStatus.js`), lead-time startup caches, AG-Grid server-side pagination, DigitalOcean Spaces photos. **Already tuned for the Supabase pooler** (`pool.min:0, idle:10s, evict:5s, keepAlive:true`) — see [`docs/verification/supabase-pooler-idle-connection-drop-20260623.md`](docs/verification/supabase-pooler-idle-connection-drop-20260623.md). | Medium. **Best-prepared service.** Repoint DB. |
| **designflow-data-syncing** | **ETL: pulls Coldlion ERP → PLM Postgres** (item headers/details, merch groups, production orders, external vendors/customers, prepack associations). `modTime`-guarded upserts. | Yes (write) | Inbound `X-API-Key`; outbound `COLDLION_API_KEY`. Invoked by host systemd `plm-sync.timer` (Ansible-managed). | Field remapping, dedup/find-or-create by composite keys, **image upload to GCS** (`storage.googleapis.com/designflow-default-bucket`). | Medium. Repoint DB; storage decision needed. |
| **designflow-frontend** | Angular 21 SPA. AG Grid Enterprise. **MSAL Azure SSO** popup → exchanges Azure token at core for Designflow JWT (stored in `localStorage`). Calls everything through the BFF. **No `@supabase/supabase-js` today.** | n/a | Azure SSO + Designflow JWT in `Authorization`. | Client-side RFQ margin/landed-cost math (`rfq.component.ts` ~4500 lines, `equation.ts`), reference-data cache, presigned-URL uploads. | Consumer only. Changes only if we expose Supabase to it. |

**Key facts that shape the plan:**

- **All PLM DB access is standard Postgres + Sequelize.** There is **no Cloud SQL IAM/proxy/socket coupling** — repointing to Supabase is a connection-string + pool-config change, not a code rewrite. The only true GCP couplings are: Cloud Build/Cloud Run deploy, Secret Manager, VPC connector, and **data-syncing's GCS image uploads**.
- **Model duplication across repos is a real hazard.** `itemHeader`, `itemDetail`, `ProdOrderHeader/Detail`, `Factory`, `vendor`, `users`, `groups`, `merchGroup`, grid tables, etc. are each **defined separately in multiple services' Sequelize models**. Columns can drift between copies. Supabase DDL must be the **union of all columns** across every repo's copy of a model, or an ORM in one service will reference a column another service didn't create. (Already flagged in [`docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md`](docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md) line 84–86.)
- **PLM auth is a different identity system.** PLM mints its **own RS256 JWT** with `{user_id, email, role}` where role ∈ {admin, sales, designer, production, sourcing_manager, vendor}. The other three apps use **Supabase Auth** (Azure provider) and RLS keyed on `app.profile`/`app.has_app_access(...)`. These identities must be reconciled before RLS can protect any table PLM writes and another app reads.
- **Connection-limit math is a risk.** Six services, each `pool.max ≈ 22`, each running multiple Cloud Run instances, all against one Supabase project, plus three browser apps. This will exceed direct-connection limits; the **Supabase transaction pooler (pgBouncer)** and disciplined pool sizing are mandatory. The idle-connection-drop issue is already documented and fixed in `tracking`; the same settings must be applied to every service.
- **A known auth bug exists** in `designflow-backend/helpers/auth.js` (`algorithm` vs `algorithms` — the RS256 allow-list is not enforced, enabling forged-token bypass). This must be fixed **before** PLM identity is trusted by shared RLS. There is also a leaked Brevo key in git history to rotate.

### 2.3 How the other three apps use the shared DB (the pattern PLM is joining)

- **CRM (`popcrm-web`)** and **PM (`poppim-web`)** are **pure browser→Supabase SPAs**: reads through `api.*` views (`security_invoker`, RLS-enforced), writes through guarded RPCs or directly to their schema's tables. No server backend. Supabase Auth (Azure). They depend **entirely on RLS** for security.
- **DAM (`popdam3`)** is **mixed**: browser reads base tables directly, but sensitive writes/async go through **Deno edge functions** + a **Railway worker** + filesystem agents. Supabase Auth.
- All three treat `u2giants/shared-db` as the **single source of truth**, carry a read-only synced `shared-db/` mirror, and follow the **four anti-collision rules** in `AGENTS.md`.

**Implication for PLM:** PLM is the only one of the four with a heavy custom Node backend and its own JWT. It will not become a pure browser-direct app soon. The realistic near-term shape is **backend-mediated** (PLM services keep the business logic and connect to Supabase), while **shared identity tables** (companies/contacts/factories/licensors) must still be RLS-safe because CRM/PM read them browser-direct.

---

## Part 3 — Guiding principles (the "perfect balance")

### 3.1 What MOVES to Supabase
- **The data itself** — PLM tables land in the `plm` schema (and selected master data is reconciled into `core`).
- **Cross-app read contracts** — stable `api.plm_*` views/RPCs so CRM/PM/DAM can read canonical PLM item/order/licensing status without touching PLM's Node services or raw tables.
- **Row-level security** for any table exposed to a browser (directly or via `api` views).
- **Realtime** — only for user-facing status tables, and only after RLS is proven.
- **Light, set-based integrity** that is genuinely data-shaped (FKs, simple triggers for audit/`updated_at`, generated columns) — where it does not duplicate app logic.

### 3.2 What STAYS in the Node backend (do not move into Supabase RLS/Edge Functions)
- **RFQ pricing & quote state machine**, re-quote workflow, stale-quote auto-archive (`designflow-backend`).
- **Tariff cascades** (Section 301 / HTS / Trump 2025) and **AI proxying** (OpenRouter/DeepSeek/Qwen, prompt-cache routing).
- **Email (Brevo)**, **Teams/Graph notifications**, **CBP CROSS** proxy.
- **Excel bulk import**, image processing, presigned-URL file workflows.
- **Coldlion ETL** transform/dedup/reconciliation (`designflow-data-syncing`).
- **Sample-tracking state machine** and **lead-time calculation**.
- **Azure SSO exchange** and any privileged orchestration.

> Rule of thumb: **data and access-control go to Supabase; procedural/integration/business logic stays in code.** If logic needs to call an external API, send email, or run a multi-step workflow, it stays in Node.

### 3.3 Cross-app guardrails (so PLM work can't break CRM/PM/DAM, and vice-versa)
1. **Schema ownership is explicit.** PLM owns `plm.*`. Shared identity lives in `core.*` and is changed only by additive, reviewed migrations. No app writes another app's schema except through an agreed contract.
2. **All schema changes follow `AGENTS.md`**: one change in flight at a time; preview branch first; additive-by-default (expand→contract); new timestamped migration files only; `shared-db` branch+PR+merge protocol.
3. **PLM services connect with a scoped role**, not a god key. A dedicated Postgres role/`search_path` per service, least-privilege grants, and (where browser-exposed) RLS — never the service-role key in a browser.
4. **Contract tests** guard the `api.plm_*` views/RPCs: a change that breaks the shape a consumer relies on fails CI before merge.
5. **Column-parity check** across the duplicated Sequelize models, run in CI, so one service can't add a column the shared DDL lacks.
6. **Source-reference tables** (`core.*_source_ref`) preserve every original PLM id so links are reversible and dedup is auditable.

---

## Part 4 — Key decisions required before locking the plan

These four decisions change the **shape** of the staged plan. They are posed to the owner now; the rest of the open questions (Part 6) can be resolved as we go.

1. **End state of Cloud SQL** — Does Supabase *fully replace* PLM's Cloud SQL (the six services repoint and Cloud SQL is decommissioned after cutover), or does Cloud SQL stay primary while Supabase holds a *synced shared copy* for cross-app reads?
2. **PLM access pattern & identity** — Keep PLM **backend-mediated** (Node services hold the logic, connect to Supabase via a scoped service role, identity bridged to Supabase Auth) — or invest now in moving PLM toward **browser-direct** like CRM/PM?
3. **Backend hosting** — Keep the six services on **GCP Cloud Run** (repoint DB to Supabase over the public endpoint + pooler) — or move them to **Coolify/Hetzner** alongside the other apps to cut DB latency and unify ops?
4. **Canonical ownership of shared identity** (customers, contacts, factories, licensors, taxonomy) — In stage one, keep these **PLM-local in `plm.*` and link to `core.*` via source-refs** (lower risk, faster) — or **merge into `core.*` immediately** (the grand vision, higher risk)?

aiTitle: Resolve uncommitted changes in supabase pooler docs

content.text: I've written [dflow_migration_plan.md](dflow_migration_plan.md) with your full mandate captured verbatim (Part 1), the discovery findings across all nine repos (Part 2), the "perfect balance" principles and cross-app guardrails (Part 3), and a draft staged plan with test gates (Part 5). Before I refine the stages into concrete migrations, four decisions change the plan's shape:

questions.question: What should happen to PLM's GCP Cloud SQL after migration?

options.label: Replace it fully
options.description: The six PLM services repoint to Supabase as their single primary DB; Cloud SQL is decommissioned after a fallback period. One database, no ongoing sync. This is the true 'shared database' goal.

options.label: Keep as primary, sync to Supabase
options.description: Cloud SQL stays PLM's primary DB; Supabase holds a synced copy of PLM data only for cross-app reads. Lower risk, but you keep two databases and a sync pipeline forever, and don't get realtime/shared-writes.

options.label: Not sure — recommend
options.description: I'll recommend the full-replace path (it's the only one that delivers the shared-database vision) with a staged fallback, and explain the trade-off.

questions.question: How should the PLM services access Supabase, and how should identity work?

options.label: Backend-mediated (keep Node)
options.description: PLM keeps its Node backends and heavy logic; they connect to Supabase via a scoped service role. Identity bridged so PLM's Azure-derived user maps to Supabase Auth for RLS on shared tables. Lowest risk, fastest.

options.label: Move toward browser-direct
options.description: Invest now in rewriting PLM toward the CRM/PM pattern (Angular talks to Supabase directly via RLS+views+RPCs), shrinking the Node backend. Much larger effort, higher risk, but matches the other apps.

options.label: Not sure — recommend
options.description: I'll recommend backend-mediated for stages 1-5 (PLM logic is too heavy to move quickly) with optional browser-direct reads later for specific screens.

questions.question: Where should the six PLM Node services run after the DB moves to Supabase?

options.label: Stay on GCP Cloud Run
options.description: Keep current Cloud Build/Cloud Run/Secret Manager setup; just repoint the DB connection to Supabase's public endpoint + pooler. Watch cross-region latency to Supabase.

options.label: Move to Coolify/Hetzner
options.description: Relocate services next to the other apps and (potentially) closer to Supabase to cut DB latency and unify ops/governance. Larger infra change, separate from the DB migration.

options.label: Not sure — recommend
options.description: I'll recommend staying on Cloud Run for the migration itself (one variable at a time), and evaluating a host move separately afterward based on measured latency.

questions.question: How should PLM's customers/contacts/factories/licensors relate to the canonical core.* tables in stage one?

options.label: PLM-local + source-refs first
options.description: Land PLM identity data in plm.* and link to core.* via source-reference tables. Reversible, auditable, fast. Merge into core.* later as a deliberate dedup project.

options.label: Merge into core.* now
options.description: Reconcile and dedupe PLM customers/vendors/licensors into the canonical core.* tables during this migration. Delivers the unified graph sooner but is the riskiest part of the whole vision (bad merges corrupt all four apps).

options.label: Not sure — recommend
options.description: I'll recommend source-refs first (decouples the risky dedup from the DB cutover), with a separate later stage to merge into core.*.

Give me a much much more detailed rundown of both options: advantages, disadvantages, risks, etc.", "Where should the six PLM Node services run after the DB moves to Supabase?"="Stay on GCP Cloud Run", "How should PLM's customers/contacts/factories/licensors relate to the canonical core.* tables in stage one?"="PLM-local + source-refs first".
